caveats: a: No published wage-weighted AI task measurement was found for the narrowed service-provider population.

e: The injected firm-count estimate is not re-estimated here.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Research and Development in Biotechnology (except Nanobiotechnology)

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Business-to-business research-service providers paid under project, milestone, full-time-equivalent, or cost-reimbursable contracts by biopharma, device, academic, and other external customers.
lens.deviation_from_default: The NAICS definition covers biotechnology R&D generally, mixing proprietary product/IP developers with outsourced service providers. The lens retains only repeat external outsourced services so that customer recurrence and transferability are coherent.
lens.excluded_activities: Venture-backed or public therapeutic and platform developers monetizing their own intellectual property, captive corporate laboratories, university and government laboratories, grant-only work, and entities without repeat external service revenue.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market contract research, discovery-support, assay, bioinformatics, and laboratory-development providers that repeatedly sell outsourced biotechnology R&D services to external customers.

caveats: No published wage-weighted AI task measurement was found for the narrowed service-provider population.
The estimate excludes proprietary drug developers by lens but available technology evidence covers biotechnology more broadly.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit NAICS 541714 occupational staffing pattern matched to payroll, plus time-and-motion studies from contract research laboratories separating wet-lab, computational, documentation, and client-management hours.
a.rationale: AI can reduce paid effort in experimental design, protocol drafting, literature synthesis, data interpretation, quality-document preparation, and scheduling. NSF describes AI use before, during, and after experiments, but physical experimentation, scientist judgment, client interaction, and regulated review remain material; the resulting wage-weighted opportunity is bounded judgment rather than an industry measurement.

d5.bridge: BLS projects 2024 to 2034 employment for NAICS 541710, Research and Development in the Physical, Engineering, and Life Sciences, rather than constant-price, constant-quality demand for the narrowed 541714 outsourced-service lens. Converting its 8.7% ten-year growth to approximately 1.04 over five years supplies the base; it may overstate demand if employment grows through proprietary biotechnology and may understate demand if outsourcing share rises.
caveats: Employment is not service quantity and the BLS projection is at 541710, not six-digit 541714.
The contemporaneous CES series shows 541714 payroll employment but does not forecast constant-quality service demand.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year US contract-research bookings, backlog, and constant-price revenue data segmented into biotechnology discovery and development services, combined with sponsor R&D-budget surveys.
d5.rationale: BLS projects 541710 employment from 866.7 thousand in 2024 to 942.5 thousand in 2034, an 8.7% increase. This is the closest official forward indicator available, with a deliberately wide range for funding cycles and the narrower service revenue model.

caveats: Clinical-trial outsourcing is broader than the NAICS and is not a count of eligible firms.
The injected firm-count estimate is not re-estimated here.
e.change_evidence: Dun & Bradstreet, PitchBook, or PrivCo establishment-level business descriptions and revenue classifications for each LMM firm, verified by customer-contract or service-line interviews.
e.rationale: The injected LMM count covers the whole NAICS, while the narrowed lens excludes proprietary product/IP developers and non-external work. Outsourced CRO activity is economically material—an industry association reports nearly 75% of clinical trials outsourced—but no source classifies the injected LMM firms by the required revenue model; the range is a transparent eligibility judgment.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541714 ($65,745,913,502) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541714 ($77,835,466,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $506,178 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541714); class <5: 2,793 firms, avg revenue/firm $684,545 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $107,131 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 701 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,517,970 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $394,062 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 476 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,808,891 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $909,091 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 599 firms, avg revenue/firm $18,599,482 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,910,819 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 242 firms, avg revenue/firm $94,118,694 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $14,729,576 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 126 firms, avg revenue/firm $297,425,317 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $46,547,062 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 599

caveats: The estimate concerns the current service basket, not future AI-native laboratory software offerings.
Highly standardized computational assays could have a materially lower operator requirement than bespoke wet-lab programs.
o.change_evidence: Service-line revenue histories from cloud laboratories and CROs showing which experiments became self-service, paired with client interviews on accountability, validation, and acceptance requirements.
o.rationale: Even with AI-enabled design, monitoring, and analysis, outsourced biotechnology work retains accountable operators for experimental execution, instrument control, data integrity, client-specific interpretation, and biosafety. NSF's cloud-lab description still centers laboratories and equipment, while NIST identifies biotechnology-specific misuse risks that support continuing oversight; the operator-required share is bounded judgment.

caveats: Medpace is a public clinical-research company and is not a representative sample of LMM biotechnology R&D service firms.
Provider mix between discovery work and clinical work can materially change pass-through behavior.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level pricing and renewal data from acquired CROs, distinguishing fixed-fee, FTE, milestone, and cost-reimbursable work, plus win-loss and client procurement interviews.
q.rationale: The narrowed firms sell expertise and capacity, supporting some retention of lower internal cost. However, a representative CRO filing recognizes service revenue over contract progress using incurred costs and includes reimbursable out-of-pocket and investigator-site costs, indicating contracts can transmit cost structures and reduce durable provider retention. The range is a billing-model judgment, not a measured NAICS margin effect.

caveats: NSF describes a funded enabling initiative, not realized productivity at LMM firms.
Adoption differs sharply between computational providers and wet-lab providers.
rho.change_evidence: Measured AI deployment outcomes from CRO laboratory-information-management-system vendors, audited validation records, and interviews with laboratory operations and quality leaders at lower-middle-market providers.
rho.rationale: Cloud-laboratory investment shows an implementation path, yet the same sources imply instruments, validation, data quality, safety controls, and workflow integration are prerequisites. Biosecurity controls cited by NIST add approval and governance friction, so only part of exposed work is expected to be operationalized in five years.

caveats: IQVIA's global disclosed life-sciences deal data are not LMM service-provider transfers.
Transfers can occur through asset sales or sponsor recapitalizations not captured in public deal datasets.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census from GF Data, S&P Capital IQ, PitchBook, or Axial filtered to US outsourced biotechnology research-service firms with normalized EBITDA in the lens band, supplemented by owner succession interviews.
s5.rationale: A five-year qualifying control-transfer rate cannot be observed from disclosed biopharma deal totals because those data emphasize larger asset and company deals. IQVIA reports 2025 M&A volume fell 7% and overall deal activity fell 12%, supporting a cautious midpoint rather than assuming a broad exit wave; the value is a bounded judgment for eligible service firms only.

principal_driver: Repeat outsourced biotechnology research work has meaningful AI assistance potential while still requiring expert laboratory accountability.
principal_weakness: The NAICS mixes proprietary biotech developers with service providers, and public evidence does not isolate LMM service-firm eligibility or control transfers.
